user_prompt: A user requested deletion of all their data. Generate the DSAR-deletion checklist including which systems to scrub.
actual_output:
**GDPR Right to Erasure (Article 17) – DSAR Deletion Checklist**

*Disclaimer: This is compliance guidance, not legal advice. Consult qualified legal counsel or your Data Protection Officer (DPO) for binding opinions on specific cases. Deletion decisions must be made on a case-by-case basis after verifying applicability of Article 17(3) exceptions (e.g., legal obligations, public interest, archiving purposes).*

### 1. Request Intake & Verification (Articles 12 & 17)
- [ ] Confirm receipt and log the request with date, requester details (without storing unnecessary new personal data), and unique reference ID.
- [ ] Verify the identity of the data subject using proportionate methods (e.g., email confirmation + additional identifier if high-risk account). Document the verification steps.
- [ ] Acknowledge the request without undue delay and within one month (extendable by two further months for complex cases per Article 12(3)). Provide transparent information on next steps.
- [ ] Assess applicability: Determine if erasure is required or if an exception applies (e.g., ongoing contract performance, legal retention obligations under tax/AML laws, or public health reasons). **Flag for DPO/legal review if exceptions may apply.**
- [ ] Check for linked rights (e.g., if this follows a prior access request under Article 15).

**Risk priority:** High – Failure to respond appropriately can trigger complaints to supervisory authorities and fines up to 4% of annual global turnover (Article 83(5)).

### 2. Data Mapping & Location Inventory (Articles 5(2), 25, 30 & 17)
Before scrubbing, maintain or reference an up-to-date Record of Processing Activities (RoPA) and data flow map. Identify **all** locations of the individual’s personal data (names, emails, usage analytics, derived data, logs, backups).

**Systems to Scrub (typical SaaS/organizational inventory – adapt to your data flow map):**
- **Production Databases & Application Storage:** Main customer database, user profiles, account records, transaction history.
- **Backup & Disaster Recovery Systems:** All full/incremental backups (note: technical limitations may allow deferred deletion – document this per Article 5(2) accountability).
- **CRM / Customer Management Platforms** (e.g., Salesforce, HubSpot, Zendesk): Contact records, support tickets, interaction history.
- **Marketing & Communications Tools:** Email marketing lists (Mailchimp, Braze), newsletters, consent/preference databases.
- **Analytics & Tracking Platforms:** Usage analytics (Google Analytics, Mixpanel, internal BI tools), event logs, behavioral profiles.
- **Logging, Monitoring & Security Systems:** Application logs, audit trails, SIEM tools, access logs that contain identifiable data.
- **File Storage & Document Repositories:** Cloud storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob), shared drives, exported reports containing the data.
- **Third-Party Data Processors & Sub-Processors:** Any vendors storing or processing on your behalf (e.g., payment processors, hosting providers, analytics subcontractors). Must notify per Article 28(4) and Article 19.
- **Employee/Admin Copies:** Local downloads, email archives, shared team folders, development/staging environments, test datasets.
- **Archival or “Deleted” Holding Areas:** Soft-delete tables, recycle bins, long-term archives (unless exception applies).
- **Other Emerging Locations:** AI/ML training datasets, data lakes, shadow IT systems, or integrated third-party SaaS tools identified in your data inventory.

**Action:** Cross-reference against your Article 30 RoPA. If no current data flow map exists, this constitutes a material compliance gap under Articles 5(2) and 30 – prioritize remediation.

### 3. Execution of Deletion (Articles 17, 19, 28 & 5(1)(f))
- [ ] Apply “erasure” per the definition in Article 4(2) – permanent, irreversible deletion or anonymization where deletion is technically impossible.
- [ ] Execute deletion across **all identified systems** in a coordinated manner (use scripts/tickets with audit trail).
- [ ] For backups: Implement a retention-based approach where immediate overwriting is not feasible; ensure data is no longer processed after restoration would occur.
- [ ] Notify all recipients to whom data has been disclosed (Article 19), including processors, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. Document recipients notified.
- [ ] Cascade deletion instructions to all Article 28 data processors via formal notice and obtain confirmation of completion.
- [ ] Anonymize or delete derived data, logs, and backups containing the data subject’s identifiers.
- [ ] Update any consent/preference databases to prevent re-collection.

**Best practice (not strict legal requirement):** Use pseudonymization or irreversible hashing during transition where full deletion is delayed for technical reasons.

### 4. Documentation & Accountability (Articles 5(2), 12(3) & 30)
- [ ] Record the full process: verification steps, legal basis assessment,
judge_reasoning: The chatbot provided a comprehensive and detailed checklist that directly addressed the user's request while adhering to its persona and safety constraints.
